title: Impact of Disorder Dynamics and Multi-Domain Kinetics on the Sliding Ferroelectricity of CVD-Grown 3R-WSe2 Bilayers
authors: Sourav Paul, Prasenjit Ghosh, Krishna Prasad Maity, Vineet Pandey, Abhijith M. B., Premananda Chatterjee, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Nicholas R. Glavin, Ajit K. Roy, Atindra Nath Pal, Vidya Kochat
abstract: Sliding ferroelectricity in van der Waals (vdW) layered systems has emerged as a promising route toward non-volatile nanoscale devices, where interlayer displacement in non-centrosymmetric bilayers generates an out-of-plane polarization. In particular, 3R-stacked bilayer transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) grown via chemical vapor deposition (CVD) have been shown to host such polarization due to broken inversion symmetry. However, a detailed investigation of the 2D ferroelectric (FE) properties of CVD-grown 2D films, particularly the role of intrinsic disorder, such as structural defects and domain structure, remains poorly understood. Here, we investigate the FE switching characteristics of CVD-grown 3R-stacked WSe2 using a graphene-based ferroelectric field-effect transistor (graphene-FE-FET) architecture, where graphene serves as a highly sensitive probe of induced charge modulation due to polarization switching of FEs. We show that the growth-induced structural disorder significantly impacts polarization switching, while multi-domain kinetics governs the evolution of the FE response. These findings provide important insights into the design and optimization of FE devices based on vdW materials.
summary: This work uses a graphene-FET setup to study ferroelectricity in 2D WSe2, revealing that while polarization switching is present, the measured electrical response is heavily influenced by structural disorder and charge trapping. The study distinguishes between intrinsic FE switching and disorder-dominated effects, which manifest as a temperature-dependent transition between hysteresis and anti-hysteresis.
main_problem: The study investigates the ferroelectric (FE) switching characteristics of CVD-grown 3R-stacked WSe2 bilayers, focusing on how intrinsic structural disorder and multi-domain kinetics affect polarization switching.
main_result: The research demonstrates that while FE switching is observable via hysteresis in FET measurements, the response is significantly complicated by disorder, leading to transitions between conventional hysteresis and anti-hysteresis depending on temperature and sweep rate.
method: The authors used a graphene-based ferroelectric field-effect transistor (graphene-FE-FET) architecture to probe the polarization switching of WSe2 bilayers at cryogenic temperatures.
model_or_system: The physical system is the 3R-stacked WSe2 bilayer grown by CVD, which exhibits sliding ferroelectricity. The device structure is a Graphene/hBN/3R-WSe2 heterostructure, where graphene acts as a highly sensitive charge probe.
key_observables: Hysteresis in the R-V_bg curve, shift in the Charge Neutrality Point (CNP) resistance peak, and the transition between hysteresis and anti-hysteresis behavior.
important_parameters: Low temperatures (e.g., 1.6 K), gate voltage sweep rate, and temperature dependence (showing transitions around 80 K and 200 K).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed hysteresis is due to FE switching, but later notes that trap-assisted effects (Interfacial Charge Layer formation) can obscure or dominate this intrinsic switching.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the atomic stacking, device fabrication, R-V_bg hysteresis loops at low T, and temperature-dependent sweeps showing the transition from hysteresis to anti-hysteresis due to trap dynamics.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the basic FE switching signature (hysteresis) at low T, to analyzing the temperature dependence revealing the competition between FE switching and charge trapping, and finally detailing the role of domain kinetics in causing apparent anti-hysteresis.
why_interesting: The interplay between intrinsic polarization switching (a non-equilibrium, ordered state) and disorder-induced charge trapping/relaxation dynamics provides a rich platform for studying complex, non-equilibrium many-body physics relevant to open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 (a) Atomic arrangement of bilayer WSe2 with different stacking configurations. While Hexagonal (H) stacking preserves inversion symmetry, a Rhombohedral (R) stacked WSe2 bilayer with broken inversion symmetry exhibits out-of-plane polarization in opposite directions for the two different stacking sequences (−P for AB and +P for BA). (b) Schematic representation of Graphene/hBN/3R-WSe2 heterostructure on SiO2/Si substrate for graphene-FE-FET device. (c) Optical image of graphene/hBN/3R-WSe2 heterostructure for device D1. (d) Resistance (R) of graphene (device D1) as a function of bottom gate voltage (Vbg) at temperature T = 1.6 K for different sweep ranges of Vbg with scan rate 200...

Fig. 2 (a) The Landau fan diagram for device D1 at T= 1.6 K illustrates the longitudinal resistance, Rxx, plotted against the magnetic field on the vertical axis and the gate bias on the horizontal axis for both the forward (top) and backward (bottom) sweeps. Different filling factor values are marked in white dashed lines. (b) Rxx as a function of Vbg voltage sweep for both forward and backward scans at B = 5 T for device D1. (c) The schematic illustrates the formation of the interfacial charge layer at the hBN-WSe2 interface. (d) A schematic of band diagram for graphene/hBN/3R-WSe2 heterostructure shows the formation of localized defect states near the valence band (VB) maxima of WSe2.

Fig. 3 (a) Hysteresis and anti-hysteresis in transfer characteristics of device D1 as a function of Vbg with scan rate 200 V/hr for different temperatures. (b) △Vd as a function of temperature. (c-d) Hysteresis, and anti-hysteresis observed in transfer characteristics for different sweep ranges of Vbg with fixed scan rate of 200 V/hr at temperature (c) T = 40 K and (d) T = 100 K. (e) Illustration of trap-sensitive electrostatic model for R −Vbg characteristics of D1 at 125 K.

Fig. 4 (a) R−Vbg characteristics as a function of Vbg at 1.6 K for different gate voltage scan rates for device D2. (b,c) Transfer characteristics for device (b) D2 and (c) D3 at different temperatures. Inset: Optical image of devices D2 and D3, respectively. Black arrow marks in fig. (a) and (c) indicate the sudden resistances jumps in graphene transport due to the relaxation of domains in 3R-WSe2.
